content.full_text:
A-28-180

February 3, 1978


Mr. Kirk Alan Pessner
Paralegal
Dobbs & Nielsen
Suite 2300, Alcoa Building
One Maritime Plaza
San Francisco, California 94111

Dear Mr. Pessner:

In response to your letter of December 19th I will reply to your
statements as they are numbered in your letter.

1. If two or more candidates are supported by the same committee
which is required to file an abbreviated campaign statement
as a result of contributing $250 or more to one or more candi-
dates in the same jurisdiction, both contributions should be
included on the same abbreviated campaign statement provided
the filing officer is the same.

2. Your representation is correct. All contributions made in a
jurisdiction where a committee has an obligation to file an
abbreviated statement should be included on the report whether
or not they are $250 or more.

3. No statement need be filed unless an individual candidate or
committee receives $250 or more.

4. You are correct. An abbreviated campaign statement must be
filed only in jurisdiction A, where the $300 contribution is
made, assuming the contribution to one candidate is $250 or
more. The contributions made to B and C need not be included
on the abbreviated campaign statement filed in jurisdiction A
since Government Code Section 84204.1(b) requires that an
abbreviated campaign statement itemize only those expenditures
which are directly related to the election for which the state-
ment is being filed. Jurisdictions B and C, where contributions
of less than $250 have been made, need not receive an abbreviated
campaign statement.

Mr. Nick Alan Pessnar                          -2-                          February 3, 1978


5. If a political action committee contributes $1,000 to a candidate
and the closing date for a semi-annual statement occurs prior to
the closing date for a campaign statement required to be filed
in connection with an election, the $1,000 contribution must be
included on a semi-annual campaign statement. The political
action committee need not report the $1,000 contribution on any
subsequent campaign statement filed.

6. The $300 must be reported on an abbreviated statement. Any
contribution which has already been reported on a semi-annual
(off-year) campaign statement need not be repeated on any cam-
paign statement it files; however, the cumulative total of
contributions ($300) should be carried forward to campaign state-
ments as necessary.

7. Recipient committees must cumulate contributions made and received
on abbreviated campaign statements. Major Donor and Expenditure
Committees do not receive contributions and therefore there are
no cumulative contribution totals to be carried forward. However,
contributions made must be cumulated for the calendar year.

8. Recipient committees which participate exclusively in special
elections forward cumulative totals from their abbreviated cam-
paign statements to their next required semi-annual campaign
statement. In other words, contributions of $50 or more received
from a single source reported on the abbreviated campaign state-
ment filed in February and March, would be repeated again on the
semi-annual campaign statement with a closing date of June 30.
The next semi-annual statement will include cumulative totals
from the July 31 report and so on.

The semi-annual campaign statement will also contain the abbreviated
campaign statement's itemization of contributions received and expendi-
tures made. This information may be incorporated by reference provided
the abbreviated and semi-annual campaign statements are filed with the
same filing officer.

I hope these comments are helpful.

Very truly yours,


Regina Siciliano-Kutschina
Technical Assistance Program
Coordinator

RSK:bw

DOBBS & NIELSEN
Attorneys and Counselors at Law
SUITE 2500, THE ALCOA BUILDING
ONE MARITIME PLAZA
SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94111

TELEPHONE (415) 362-1940

December 19, 1977


Ms. Regina Kutchins
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Abbreviated Campaign Statements (Form 480)

Dear Regina:

     In conversations you and I had in October,
November, and December, you gave me certain verbal advice
regarding the abbreviated campaign statement (Form 480) and
its uses. I would like to review with you what I understood
your responses to be. I would appreciate your clarification
on any of these points as we would like to advise our clients
on the proper use of the Abbreviated Campaign Statement.

     1) If two or more candidates are supported by
the same committee in the same jurisdiction and both candi-
dates supported have received over $250 from the committee,
both candidates must be reported on the same Abbreviated
Campaign Statement;

     2) If two or more candidates are supported by
the same committee, in the same jurisdiction, and only one
candidate received over $250 from the committee, all candi-
dates supported in that jurisdiction must be reported on the
same Abbreviated Campaign Statement;

     3) If several candidates are supported by the
same committee, and contributions to each individual candidate
are not over $250, then nothing need be filed even though
the aggregate contribution by the committee is over $250.

     4) If, for example, $300 is given in Jurisdiction
A, $100 in Jurisdiction B, and $50 in Jurisdiction C, the
committee need only file an Abbreviated Campaign Statement
in Jurisdiction A and does not have to disclose contributions
in other jurisdictions.




SACRAMENTO OFFICE - SUITE 544, 1127 ELEVENTH STREET, SACRAMENTO, CA 95814 • (916) 446-6752

Ms. Regina Kutchins
December 19, 1977
Page two


     5)  If a political action committee gives $1,000
to a candidate in Jurisdiction A, and reports the contribution
on its off-year statement, the PAC does not have to file an
Abbreviated Campaign Statement if the election campaign filing
deadlines are after a regular reporting deadline;

     6)  If a political action committee gives $1,000
now and reports it on an off-year statement, and then gives
$300 in Jurisdiction A prior to a normal filing deadline,
the PAC must file an Abbreviated Statement for that jurisdiction,
but must only report the $300.

     7)  Cumulative amounts must be shown for recipient
committees on the Abbreviated Campaign Statement, even though
past contributions were reported on the off-year statement.
This does not apply to major donors and expenditure committee
filers whose cumulatives cut off annually.

     8)  Recipient committees whose sole purpose is to
participate in special elections, (and thus required to file
the Abbreviated Campaign Statements in each case), must
continue to cumulate the amounts given to any candidate in a
particular jurisdiction, because that committee will never
be allowed to file a Final Campaign Statement.

     I would appreciate your comments on these points
at your earliest convenience, so that we may advise our
clients accordingly.  Thank you for your assistance.

                                        Kind Regards,


                                        Kirk Alan Pessner,
                                        Paralegal

KAP:kad

sections.question: 1) If two or more candidates are supported by the same committee in the same jurisdiction and both candidates supported have received over $250 from the committee, both candidates must be reported on the same Abbreviated Campaign Statement; 2) If two or more candidates are supported by the same committee, in the same jurisdiction, and only one candidate received over $250 from the committee, all candidates supported in that jurisdiction must be reported on the same Abbreviated Campaign statement; 3) If several candidates are supported by the same committee, and contributions to each individual candidate are not over $250, then nothing need be filed even though the aggregate contribution by the committee is over $250. 4) If, for example, $300 is given in Jurisdiction A, $100 in Jurisdiction B, and $50 in Jurisdiction C, the committee need only file an Abbreviated Campaign statement in Jurisdiction A and does not have to disclose contributions in other jurisdictions. 5) If a political action Committee gives $1,000 to a Candidate in Jurisdiction A, and reports the contribution on its off-year statement, the PAC does not have to file an Abbreviated Campaign statement if the election campaign filing deadlines are after a regular reporting deadline; 6) If a political action committee gives $1,000 now and reports it on an off-year statement, and then gives $300 in Jurisdiction A prior to a normal filing deadline, the PAC must file an Abbreviated Statement for that jurisdiction, but must only report the $300. 7) Cumulative amounts must be shown for recipient committees on the Abbreviated Campaign Statement, even though past contributions were reported on the off-year statement. This does not apply to major donors and expenditure committee filers whose cumulatives cut off annually; 8) Recipient committees whose sole purpose is to participate in special elections, (and thus required to file the Abbreviated Campaign Statements in each case), must continue to cumulate the amounts given to any candidate in a particular jurisdiction, because that committee will never be allowed to file a Final Campaign Statement.
sections.conclusion: The FPPC Technical Assistance Program Coordinator provides detailed point-by-point responses confirming and clarifying the requester's understanding of Abbreviated Campaign Statement rules, including guidance on multi-candidate reporting, $250 thresholds, jurisdiction-specific filing, reporting of contributions already disclosed on semi-annual statements, and cumulative total requirements for different committee types.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks for clarification on multiple technical issues regarding the proper use and filing of Abbreviated Campaign Statements (Form 480), including which candidates and contributions must be reported together, when filing is required, jurisdiction-specific filing obligations, and cumulative reporting requirements for different types of committees.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirms that abbreviated campaign statements must report all contributions made in a jurisdiction where the threshold is met regardless of individual candidate amounts; contributions already reported on semi-annual statements need not be repeated but cumulative totals must be carried forward; and recipient committees in special elections must cumulate contributions by candidate and jurisdiction for future semi-annual filings.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both incoming request (December 19, 1977 letter from Kirk Alan Pessnett) and FPPC response (February 3, 1978 letter from Regina Kutchins). The document_type in the input metadata states 'advice_letter' with year 1978, but the actual response is dated February 3, 1978 and the incoming request is from December 19, 1977. Minor OCR errors present in document text (e.g., 'Pessnexz' for 'Pessnett', 'Alkoa' for 'Alcoa'). The response is numbered 1-8 with detailed guidance on each point raised.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1) If two or more candidates are supported by the same committee in the same jurisdiction and both candidates supported have received over $250 from the committee, both candidates must be reported on the same Abbreviated Campaign Statement; 2) If two or more candidates are supported by the same committee, in the same jurisdiction, and only one candidate received over $250 from the committee, all candidates supported in that jurisdiction must be reported on the same Abbreviated Campaign statement; 3) If several candidates are supported by the same committee, and contributions to each individual candidate are not over $250, then nothing need be filed even though the aggregate contribution by the committee is over $250. 4) If, for example, $300 is given in Jurisdiction A, $100 in Jurisdiction B, and $50 in Jurisdiction C, the committee need only file an Abbreviated Campaign statement in Jurisdiction A and does not have to disclose contributions in other jurisdictions. 5) If a political action Committee gives $1,000 to a Candidate in Jurisdiction A, and reports the contribution on its off-year statement, the PAC does not have to file an Abbreviated Campaign statement if the election campaign filing deadlines are after a regular reporting deadline; 6) If a political action committee gives $1,000 now and reports it on an off-year statement, and then gives $300 in Jurisdiction A prior to a normal filing deadline, the PAC must file an Abbreviated Statement for that jurisdiction, but must only report the $300. 7) Cumulative amounts must be shown for recipient committees on the Abbreviated Campaign Statement, even though past contributions were reported on the off-year statement. This does not apply to major donors and expenditure committee filers whose cumulatives cut off annually; 8) Recipient committees whose sole purpose is to participate in special elections, (and thus required to file the Abbreviated Campaign Statements in each case), must continue to cumulate the amounts given to any candidate in a particular jurisdiction, because that committee will never be allowed to file a Final Campaign Statement.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC Technical Assistance Program Coordinator provides detailed point-by-point responses confirming and clarifying the requester's understanding of Abbreviated Campaign Statement rules, including guidance on multi-candidate reporting, $250 thresholds, jurisdiction-specific filing, reporting of contributions already disclosed on semi-annual statements, and cumulative total requirements for different committee types.

embedding.summary: This is an FPPC advice letter responding to detailed questions from a paralegal at Cobra & Nielsen regarding the proper completion and filing of Abbreviated Campaign Statements, addressing multi-candidate reporting, threshold requirements, jurisdiction-specific obligations, and cumulative reporting for various committee types.
